pages.title: Welcome to Safe Internet & Link Safety
pages.subtitle: Learn why link safety matters, how suspicious links can be spotted, why shortened links are tricky, and what habits help people browse more safely.

video.caption: Introduction to safe internet browsing and link safety

pages.title: Why link safety matters
pages.text:
Many cyber incidents begin when someone clicks a malicious link. A link may look harmless, but it can lead to a fake login page, a malware download, or a page designed to trick someone into sharing sensitive information.

That is why link safety matters so much. One rushed click can expose passwords, company data, or an entire device.

A suspicious link is often the first step in a larger attack.

Warning: The example links below are for training purposes only. Do not try to visit or test suspicious-looking links. Website names and destinations can change over time.

Example suspicious linkWhy it is not safe
micrоsoft-support.comThe underlined о is meant to represent a Cyrillic look-alike character, not a normal English o. A link can look correct at a glance while still being fake.
micr0soft-login.comThe underlined 0 replaces the letter o. This is a number/letter swap designed to fool someone reading too quickly.
paypaI-secure.comThe underlined I is a capital letter look-alike replacing a lowercase l. That small visual change can be easy to miss.
microsoft-login-security.comThe underlined extra words make the site sound official, but they do not make it legitimate. Attackers often add trusted-sounding terms to fake domains.
microsoft.com.security-check.example.comThe underlined section shows the real problem: this is a misleading subdomain. People may stop reading after seeing microsoft.com, but the real domain is at the end.
paypal-login.coThe underlined ending uses a different top-level domain. A site may look familiar but still use the wrong ending.
bit.ly/urgent-account-fixThe underlined part is hidden behind a shortened link. You cannot clearly see the real destination before clicking.
faceboook-login.comThe underlined extra letters are an example of typosquatting. Attackers slightly misspell trusted names so the mistake is easy to overlook.

pages.title: Hover before you click
pages.text:
One of the easiest safety habits is to hover over a link before clicking it. Many email programs and browsers show the real destination when you place your cursor over the link.

If the destination looks unfamiliar, misspelled, overly long, or unrelated to the sender, stop and double-check before doing anything else.

Example:

  • Displayed text: Reset your Microsoft password
  • Real destination when hovering: login-security-check.example-site.com

Reference: CISA: Teach Employees to Avoid Phishing

pages.title: Why shortened links are harder to judge
pages.text:
Shortened links make link safety more difficult because they hide the real destination. Instead of seeing the full website address, you may only see a short service link.

Examples: bit.ly, tinyurl.com, and shortened links used on social media or in messages.

That does not mean every shortened link is malicious, but it does mean you have less information before clicking. This makes it easier for attackers to hide where a link really goes.

When a link hides its destination, slow down and verify before clicking.

pages.title: Look-alike domains
pages.text:
Attackers often create websites that look very similar to legitimate ones. These are sometimes called look-alike domains or spoofed links.

  • micr0soft.com instead of microsoft.com
  • paypaI.com using a capital I as in Ice instead of a lowercase l as in lima
  • company-login-support.com instead of the organization’s real domain

Always read the address carefully before entering credentials or payment information.

pages.title: HTTPS helps, but it is not enough
pages.text:
Many legitimate websites use HTTPS, which helps protect data moving between your browser and the site.

You may also see a lock icon in the browser address bar. That is helpful, but it does not prove the site is trustworthy. A fake website can still use HTTPS.

Good rule: HTTP = bad, HTTPS = good. Treat HTTPS as one helpful sign, not final proof that a site is safe.

image.alt: HTTPS and browser lock icon example

pages.title: Pop-ups, fake warnings, and surprise downloads
pages.text:
Unsafe browsing is not only about links in email. It can also involve pop-ups, fake warnings, and online ads that try to scare or rush you into clicking.

The FBI recommends using pop-up blockers and being cautious with unexpected warnings, downloads, and pop-up ads. This matters even more when you are using a company computer or connected to a company network, because one bad click can put work systems, accounts, or shared data at risk.

  • Do not click inside a suspicious pop-up, even if it claims your device is infected.
  • Do not click pop-up ads or urgent warning ads just because they look professional or “safe.”
  • Do not download software or browser extensions from unexpected alerts.
  • Be extra cautious on a company machine or company network, where unsafe browsing can affect more than one device.

If a page is pressuring you, asking for a download, or trying to frighten you into acting fast, stop and report it through a trusted source instead of interacting with it.

A pop-up that looks helpful or urgent can still be malicious. Do not trust it just because it appears professional.

Reference: FBI guidance on pop-ups and malware

image.alt: Example of a pop-up or ad that may look safe but should not be trusted automatically

pages.title: Safe link habits that actually help
pages.text:
Good link safety is mostly about small habits followed consistently.

HabitWhy it helps
Hover before clickingLets you inspect the real destination first
Read the full domain carefullyHelps catch spoofed or look-alike sites
Avoid rushed clickingReduces mistakes caused by urgency or fear
Type known websites yourself when possibleSafer for well-known sites because it avoids relying on links from messages, ads, or pop-ups
Use trusted bookmarksHelps you return to sites you already know instead of clicking fresh links each time
Pause before downloadingPrevents unsafe files or software from being opened

Reference: CISA: Recognize and Report Phishing

image.alt: Safe browsing practices and link-checking habits

pages.title: What to do if you already clicked
pages.text:
Sometimes people click before realizing something is wrong. What matters next is responding quickly.

  1. Stop interacting with the page, download, or pop-up.
  2. Do not enter credentials or payment information.
  3. Close the page if possible.
  4. Report it quickly to IT, security, a supervisor, or your organization’s reporting contact.
  5. Tell them what happened, including what you clicked and whether you entered any information.

Quick reporting can keep one bad click from becoming a larger incident.

pages.title: Ready for the quiz?
pages.subtitle: This quiz will test your understanding of link safety, suspicious links, shortened URLs, and safe browsing habits.
